title: Northern Pacific Sea Star Control Measures
text:
The reading passage proposes three methods to control the spread of the invasive Northern Pacific sea star: using quicklime, introducing natural parasites, and sterilizing ballast water. However, the lecturer argues that none of these measures is likely to be effective and challenges each point made in the reading.

First, the reading claims that quicklime can kill invasive sea stars without harming commercially valuable mollusks. The lecturer counters that this does not mean quicklime is safe for the overall ecosystem. According to her, quicklime also kills native sea stars, which play an essential role in maintaining ecological balance. Because quicklime remains active on the seafloor for a long time, native sea stars entering the treated areas after the invasive sea stars die will also be poisoned. Consequently, using quicklime may seriously damage the local ecosystem rather than protect it.

Second, the reading suggests introducing parasites that naturally limit sea star populations in their native habitats. In contrast, the lecturer explains that these parasites are not effective enough to significantly reduce the invasive population. The parasites do not fully destroy the reproductive systems of male sea stars, meaning infected males can still fertilize eggs. Since a single female can produce up to 20 million eggs, the lecturer argues that introducing parasites will barely reduce the overall population and therefore will not solve the problem.

Finally, the reading argues that sterilizing ballast water on ships would prevent larvae from being transported to distant areas. The lecturer refutes this by pointing out that ballast water is not the only means of dispersal. Sea star larvae can attach themselves to a variety of surfaces, such as the bottoms of small boats and fishing equipment. Because the larvae cling to many types of objects that are moved between regions, sterilizing ballast water alone cannot stop the spread of the species.

In conclusion, while the reading provides three seemingly promising solutions, the lecturer demonstrates that each method has major limitations and is unlikely to effectively prevent the continued spread of the Northern Pacific sea star.
